title: Análisis Interactivo del Titanic
text:
En esta página exploramos el dataset Titanic y construimos un modelo de Machine Learning (Random Forest) para predecir la supervivencia.





Code
library(tidyverse)


Warning: package 'tidyverse' was built under R version 4.3.3


Warning: package 'dplyr' was built under R version 4.3.3


── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.4
✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.5.0
✔ ggplot2   3.5.2     ✔ tibble    3.2.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.3     ✔ tidyr     1.3.0
✔ purrr     1.0.2     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors


Code
library(plotly)



Attaching package: 'plotly'

The following object is masked from 'package:ggplot2':

    last_plot

The following object is masked from 'package:stats':

    filter

The following object is masked from 'package:graphics':

    layout


Code
library(DT)

titanic <- as.data.frame(Titanic)

titanic <- titanic %>%
  uncount(Freq) %>%
  mutate(
    Survived = factor(Survived),
    Sex = factor(Sex),
    Class = factor(Class),
    Age = factor(Age)
  )

datatable(head(titanic, 20),
          options = list(pageLength = 5),
          caption = "Vista previa del dataset Titanic")














Code
plot_ly(
  titanic,
  x = ~Class,
  color = ~Survived,
  type = "bar"
) %>%
  layout(
    barmode = "stack",
    title = "Supervivencia por Clase",
    yaxis = list(title = "Conteo")
  )


Warning in RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(N, "Set2"): minimal value for n is 3, returning requested palette with 3 different levels

Warning in RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(N, "Set2"): minimal value for n is 3, returning requested palette with 3 different levels













Code
library(randomForest)


Warning: package 'randomForest' was built under R version 4.3.3


randomForest 4.7-1.1


Type rfNews() to see new features/changes/bug fixes.



Attaching package: 'randomForest'


The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr':

    combine


The following object is masked from 'package:ggplot2':

    margin


Code
set.seed(123)

train_index <- sample(1:nrow(titanic), 0.7*nrow(titanic))
train <- titanic[train_index, ]
test  <- titanic[-train_index, ]

rf_model <- randomForest(
  Survived ~ Class + Sex + Age,
  data = train,
  ntree = 200,
  importance = TRUE
)

rf_model



Call:
 randomForest(formula = Survived ~ Class + Sex + Age, data = train,      ntree = 200, importance = TRUE) 
               Type of random forest: classification
                     Number of trees: 200
No. of variables tried at each split: 1

        OOB estimate of  error rate: 21.17%
Confusion matrix:
      No Yes class.error
No  1030  25  0.02369668
Yes  301 184  0.62061856








Code
importance_df <- as.data.frame(importance(rf_model))
importance_df$Variable <- rownames(importance_df)

plot_ly(
  importance_df,
  x = ~MeanDecreaseGini,
  y = ~reorder(Variable, MeanDecreaseGini),
  type = "bar",
  orientation = "h"
) %>%
  layout(
    title = "Importancia de Variables",
    xaxis = list(title = "Mean Decrease Gini"),
    yaxis = list(title = "")
  )












Code
pred <- predict(rf_model, newdata = test)

conf_matrix <- table(Predicho = pred,
                     Real = test$Survived)

datatable(as.data.frame(conf_matrix),
          caption = "Matriz de Confusión")











Sexo y Clase son variables clave.
Random Forest tiene buena capacidad predictiva.
La interactividad mejora el análisis.
